critic_raw:
You are reviewing an ML project. A document AI startup developed a vision-language model to extract totals, merchant names, dates, and tax amounts from retail receipts...

CRITIQUE:

The evaluation contains two structural flaws that prevent the 8.4 pp accuracy gap from being accepted as clean evidence of model superiority.

1. [VENDOR-CONFIG] VENDOR BASELINE DEFAULT CONFIGURATION ONLY: The commercial OCR product was run with its default general-document configuration. The vendor offers a receipt-specific parser in its enterprise tier — a configuration purpose-built for the exact task being evaluated. Running the baseline in a demonstrably sub-optimal mode without acknowledging this as a limitation means the comparison benchmarks against the vendor's generic setup, not its best available capability for this use case. The magnitude of the gap may close or disappear if the receipt-specific parser is used.

2. [LANGUAGE-MISMATCH] LANGUAGE CONFIGURATION MISMATCH: 30% of the evaluation set contains bilingual English-French Canadian receipts with province-specific tax formats. The startup fine-tuned on both English and French receipts. The commercial engine was run with English-only settings. This creates a direct language capability mismatch that would be expected to produce a measurable accuracy gap specifically on the bilingual subset, independent of any intrinsic model quality difference.

Both issues are correctable in principle: run the vendor's receipt-specific tier on the full dataset and/or with bilingual settings. Until this is done, the 8.4 pp gap is confounded by configuration choices, not model quality alone.
defender_raw:
You are reviewing an ML project. A document AI startup developed a vision-language model to extract totals, merchant names, dates, and tax amounts from retail receipts...

DEFENSE:

The evaluation design reflects realistic deployment conditions and the comparison is practically valid.

1. REALISTIC DEPLOYMENT BASELINE: Most small-business customers use the lower-cost default package, not the enterprise tier with receipt-specific parsers. The startup is selling to small businesses who use default configurations. Benchmarking against the default is the commercially relevant comparison.

2. FINE-TUNING IS LEGITIMATE: The startup has fine-tuned on English and French receipts. This is a legitimate product capability. The commercial engine's English-only configuration reflects the product tier most customers would deploy. Comparing a tuned specialized model against a general-purpose default is exactly the product value proposition.

3. ROTATED/CRUMPLED HANDLING: The startup's model handles degraded image quality better, which is a genuine quality advantage in real-world bookkeeping workflows.

4. PROCESSING TIME ACCEPTABLE: 1.7 seconds vs 0.6 seconds is a reasonable tradeoff for higher accuracy in batch bookkeeping workflows.

The comparison reflects realistic deployment conditions and the model demonstrates practical superiority for the target customer segment.
adjudication_raw: The critic raises two specific configuration issues: (1) the vendor was run in default mode, not with its receipt-specific enterprise parser; (2) 30% of the test set is bilingual but the vendor was run English-only while the startup was fine-tuned on bilingual data. The defender argues that default configuration is the relevant baseline for the target customer. Both arguments have merit for different questions: for a 'does this beat what customers currently use' question, the defender is right; for a 'is this model genuinely better' question, the critic is right. The language mismatch is particularly important because it creates a systematic accuracy deficit on 30% of the test set that has nothing to do with intrinsic model quality. This is a genuine methodological issue that could be resolved empirically: test the vendor's engine with bilingual settings and/or the receipt-specific parser on the same evaluation set. The core question — whether the gap holds under a fair comparison — is empirically answerable.

empirical_test.measure: Exact-field accuracy on the 8,400-receipt evaluation set
empirical_test.success_criterion: Startup model accuracy gap vs. vendor (with receipt-specific parser and bilingual configuration) remains ≥ 5 pp, confirming genuine model advantage
empirical_test.failure_criterion: Gap falls below 5 pp or disappears when vendor is run with receipt-specific configuration and bilingual settings, indicating the gap is primarily a configuration artifact
